coredata.dc:title: Organic acid participation strategy for the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots and their application in dual-mode determination of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shenghong
Wang, Ke
Wang, Xin
Sun, Xiaohan
coredata.dc:description: In-depth research on surface functionalization of carbon dots (CDs) has opened new horizons for their application in chemosensing and biosensing. However, the practicability of CDs is still limited due to their unmanageable fluorescence properties. In this study, organic acid was employed to regulate and control the fluorescence performance of CDs. A remarkable enhancement of fluorescence and redshift of the fluorescent band of CDs was observed via organic acids’ participation in the water phase preparation process. Furthermore, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout strategy was constructed for the analysis of copper ions (Cu2+) based on ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)-regulated carbon dots. The proposed sensing system can be used in the fast detection of Cu2+ via an obvious solution color change from yellow to brown under natural light, and the trace analysis of Cu2+ can be realized by quenching the green fluorescence band with detection limit of 21 nM. The designed sensor has been employed to the analysis of Cu2+ in water and urine samples successfully, and cell imaging experiment was performed to evaluate the feasibility of biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic acid
Carbon dots
Dual-mode sensing
Copper ion
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
Intracellular imaging of Cu2+ and fiber staining

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PL spectra of b-CDs (a) and e-CDs (b) under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: The fluorescence image of CDs water solution under 365 nm UV light.
The TEM image of e-CDs (a), the particle size distribution of e-CDs (b), the XRD pattern of e-CDs (c), and the FTIR spectra of b-CDs and e-CDs (d).
The full XPS spectrum of e-CDs (a), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).
The UV–vis absorption spectrum of e-CDs (a) and the excitation and emission spectrum of e-CDs (b).
The UV–vis absorption (a) and PL (b) spectra of e-CDs before and after added Cu2+.

The PL spectra of the e-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0.1 to 200 μM (a). Plot of ΔI/I0 vs the concentration of Cu2+ (b). The UV–vis absorption spectra of e-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ from 1 to 150 μM (c). Plot of A/A0 vs the concentration of Cu2+ (d). Error bar represent ± s.d. (n = 3).

PL intensity response (ΔI/I0) of e-CDs toward various metal ions (a). UV–vis absorption spectra of e-CDs before and after the addition of different heavy metal cations (b). The concentrations of all the heavy metal cations for fluorescence and colorimetric mode are100 μM.

Bright-field transmission images (a–c) and their corresponding fluorescent microscopy images (d–f) of SMMC 7721 hepatoma cells. Cells were untreated (a, d), cells were treated with e-CDs (b, e), cells were incubated with e-CDs and then incubated with Cu2+ (c, f).

Fluorescence images of absorbent cotton without being stained (a–c) and stained with e-CDs (d–f). The fluorescent images were collected at the excitation of UV (a, d), blue (b, c) and green (c, f) light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Synthesis of e-CDs and its application in the detection of Cu2+ in colorimetric and fluorescent mode.
The analysis results for certified reference materials.

row.entry: Number of the certified reference material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of spiked Cu2+ ions in Yellow River water and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Organic acid participation strategy for the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots and their application in dual-mode determination of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: JiNan Center for Food and Drug Control, Jinan 250102, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: JiNan Center for Food and Drug Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: JiNan Center for Food and Drug Control, Jinan 250102, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In-depth research on surface functionalization of carbon dots (CDs) has opened new horizons for their application in chemosensing and biosensing. However, the practicability of CDs is still limited due to their unmanageable fluorescence properties. In this study, organic acid was employed to regulate and control the fluorescence performance of CDs. A remarkable enhancement of fluorescence and redshift of the fluorescent band of CDs was observed via organic acids’ participation in the water phase preparation process. Furthermore, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout strategy was constructed for the analysis of copper ions (Cu2+) based on ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)-regulated carbon dots. The proposed sensing system can be used in the fast detection of Cu2+ via an obvious solution color change from yellow to brown under natural light, and the trace analysis of Cu2+ can be realized by quenching the green fluorescence band with detection limit of 21 nM. The designed sensor has been employed to the analysis of Cu2+ in water and urine samples successfully, and cell imaging experiment was performed to evaluate the feasibility of biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, as one of the essential trace elements of human body, plays a vital role in both living creature and the environment [1]. Nevertheless, abnormal concentrations of copper ions (Cu2+) in vivo can cause a series of lesion in plants and animals [2]. On the other hand, unreasonable discharge of copper-containing wastewater makes the detection of Cu2+ in environmental water samples particularly important. For this reason, it is of great research significance to develop rapid and sensitive method for the detection of Cu2+ in vivo and environmental sample. Compare to traditional analysis method of Cu2+, such as atomic emission/absorption spectroscopy [3], ion chromatography-UV–vis method [4], and electrochemical methods [5], colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout sensing system can simultaneously satisfy the need of rapid screening and quantitative analysis of Cu2+ in the environment and biosensing field. Unlike magnetic/fluorescent [6] and fluorescence/surface-enhanced Raman scattering technology [7,8] dual-readout modes, the colorimetric/fluorescent dual-readout mode has received considerable attention due to its simplicity, sensitivity and accuracy [9,10]. By using colorimetric method, the concentrations of target analyte can be determined rapidly to confirm whether they exceed the permit value [11,12], and the fluorescent assay can be used in highly sensitive quantitative analysis of target analyte [13].

Series of nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots [14], metal nanoparticles [15,16], metal-organic frameworks [17] and carbon dots (CDs) [18–20], have been applied in the construction of dual-readout sensing system to date. Among them, CDs possess significant advantages in biocompatibility, chemical inertness and excellent optical stabilities and have been widely applied in chemosensing and biosensing [21,22]. However, the construction of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout sensor based on CDs generally needs to integrate another one or more nanosensors. Shi et al. developed a dual-mode nanosensor by integrating blue CDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the quantitative analysis of glutathione based on color change of AuNPs and photoluminescence (PL) intensity change of CDs [19]. Zhao et al. developed a dual-readout protocol using silver nanoparticles and CDs that was applied to the analysis of acetylcholinesterase activity and its inhibitors [23]. These sensors are all sensitive, but in most cases, CDs can only provide fluorescent signals for sensing system, and typically require assistance from metal nanoparticles or organic chromogenic reagent for the colorimetric detection. It is still a challenge to develop single CDs that possess both visible absorption and fluorescent signal for colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout sensing of target analyte.

Although the fluorescence properties and applications of CDs have been studied in detail for years, CDs with visible color that can be used in colorimetric sensing are rare. Lin et al. have demonstrated that phenylenediamine can be used as the raw material for the preparation of CDs with different emission wavelengths [24]. The obtained CDs possess both visible color and bright fluorescence, enabling the detection of target analytes through a colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode based on single CDs. However, the preparation of these CDs generally needs the participation of organic solvents for the requirement of high quantum yield (QY), which is inconsistent with the principle of green chemistry and biosensing applications [25–27]. And it is still difficult to obtain high QY CDs through water phase synthesis. Furthermore, the flexible control of wavelength emission and fluorescent efficiency of CDs is more challenging to achieve, which is also a key factor to meet the different requirements of various analysis purposes [28]. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop simple and effective ways to adjust the fluorescent properties of CDs for extensive and suitable chemosensing and biosensing applications.

Considering the above questions, in this study, organic acid was used to regulate and control the fluorescence performance of CDs during water phase preparation, and a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout nanosensor was constructed for the detection of Cu2+. A redshift in the optimal fluorescence band and enhancement of the optical efficiency of CDs were observed via organic acid participation during preparation. Highly green-fluorescent e-CDs were prepared using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) served as raw materials using a facile hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The source materials were inexpensive, and the preparation procedure is simple. The obtained e-CDs possess good water-solubility and photostability. Cu2+ can induce obvious color and PL intensity change of e-CDs. The applicability of the proposed method was checked by quantification analysis of spiked Cu2+ in water and urine samples. In addition, the prepared CDs were also successfully applied to visualize intracellular Cu2+ in live SMMC 7721 hepatoma cells and fiber staining.

M-phenylenediamine (m-PD), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), malic acid (MA) and L-(+)-tartaric acid (TA) were received from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Two certified reference materials, GB08608 and GBW (E) 080039 were obtained from National Institute of Metrology (Beijing, China), both of which are simulated natural water. The water with resistance of 18 MΩ⋅ cm was used in the whole experiment (Direct-Q 3 UV, Hong Kong, China).

The optical characterizations of e-CDs were measured on ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV 3600, Agilent technologies Co., LTD, USA) and fluorospectrophotometer (F97Pro, Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The particle size distribution of e-CDs was measured on an EliteSizer dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) instrument (Brookhaven Instruments, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was recorded with a D8 ADVANCE03030502 X’Pert diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphology of the e-CDs was characterized by using FEI Tecnai G2 F30 (USA) transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Nexus 670 spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was characterized by using an ESCALAB250 electron spectrometer with Al Kα (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Cell and fiber microscopy images were performed using Olympus BX53 fluorescence microscopy (Japan). The MTT test was performed on an RT-6100 enzyme-mark analyzer (Shenzhen, China).

In a typical process, 0.37 g of m-PD, 0.025 g of EDTA (or TA, MA) and 20 mL of water were mixed in a conical flask and ultrasonically dissolved. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and reacted hydrothermally for 10 h at 240 °C. A dark black solution was obtained after the reaction was over. For further dialysis, the prepared fluorescent e-CDs were stored at 4 °C. The bare CDs (b-CDs) were prepared using only m-PD as raw material following the above mentioned conditions.

As a typical procedure, different concentrations Cu2+ (10 μL each) solutions were added to a mixture of 20 μL of e-CDs solution (5 mg⋅mL−1) and 2.5 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) respectively. The PL intensity of 513 nm and absorbance of 440 nm were measured after incubation for 15 min. The selectivity of the probe with other heavy metal ions including Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+ was determined following the procedure of Cu2+. The reliability of the method for the analysis of Cu2+ was evaluated through real sample analysis and cell imaging. Urine sample was collected from a male volunteer. The sample was centrifuged firstly for 10 min (12,000 rpm). Next, the sample was filtered and diluted 100-fold with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). Water sample was obtained from the Yellow River (Jinan, China) and filtered through a filter membrane (0.45 μm). The treated samples were spiked Cu2+ with different concentrations. The experiments were performed at room temperature (25 °C).

Two kinds of CDs were prepared by using similar process and designated them as b-CDs and e-CDs. The b-CDs were synthesized using only m-PD as raw material and exhibited the PL property of excitation-wavelength-dependent. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the b-CDs aqueous solution emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm light, and the optimal emission and excitation wavelength was at 425 nm and 340 nm, respectively. The b-CDs also showed green fluorescence under excitation of 440 nm, but the PL intensity was considerably lower than the blue emission band. When CDs are applied in bioimaging, the signal of blue fluorescence CDs may be interfered by the intrinsic blue fluorescence of cells and tissue [29]. Therefore, CDs with a long emission wavelength are preferred. In this study, several organic acids, including EDTA, tartaric acid and malic acid, were proposed to improve the fluorescence efficiency of the green emission band. As shown in Fig. S1, The QY of CDs obtained by EDTA (31.2%) participation is much higher than that of tartaric acid (23.7%) and malic acid (21.6%). Therefore, EDTA was selected to react with m-PD in water phase for the preparation of green fluorescence CDs (e-CDs). From Fig. 1b, we can see that the optimal emission band exhibited red shift and the fluorescence efficiency of the green emission band was clearly enhanced. And the e-CDs emitted bright green fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light. The green emission band is due to the effect of intrinsic electronic conjugate structures, whereas blue emission band is due to the effect of surface functional groups [30,31]. EDTA can enhance the green fluorescence band obviously through increasing intrinsic electronic conjugate structures, which is similar to the catalytic effect of inorganic acid [26]. Besides, from Fig. S1 we can see that the QY of green emission band of e-CD was 31.2% (using rhodamine 6G as a reference), which was much higher than that seen in the b-CDs (8.1%). The obvious improvement of QY also may be due to the coexistence of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs [32] and the acceleration effect of organic acids in the preparation process. These results indicate that organic acids can effectively adjust the optimal emission wavelength and improve the PL performance of CDs in water phase synthesis.

The morphology of e-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a, is monodisperse with a nearly spherical shape and the size distribution is approximately at 1.8–3.0 nm (Fig. 2b). As displayed in Fig. 2c, the XRD pattern of the prepared e-CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak from 15°-30°, centered at approximately 25°; no other sharp diffraction peaks were observed, which confirmed the amorphous crystal structure [33]. These results indicate that the obtained products have been fully carbonized through the high-temperature and high-pressure reaction at 240 °C.

FTIR and XPS were used to further confirm the structure and composition of the e-CDs. From Fig. 2d, the stretching vibration peaks of NH and OH can be seen at 3336 and 3215 cm−1 respectively. Furthermore, peaks at 1621 cm−1 and 1323 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CN [34]. Two bands at the wavenumbers 1384 and 1496 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic CC stretching vibration. Compared with b-CDs, although the surface functional group types of e-CDs have no change, the relative absorption intensity of CO functional groups increased significantly. The result indicated that the EDTA participation is conducive to the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups, which contributes to improving PL performance [32]. The full XPS spectrum of the e-CDs displayed three peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.1 eV, and 533.0 eV, assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The CC/CC (284.4 eV), CN/CO (285.7 eV) and CO (288.1 eV) bands in the e-CDs were observed from C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) [35]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) confirmed the presence of pyridinic Ns (398.5 eV), 1°/2° amino Ns (399.3 eV) and pyrrolic Ns (400.3 eV) [36]. The O1s (Fig. 3d) exhibited two peaks at 531.7 eV and 533.1 eV respectively, attributed to the CO and CO bands of the e-CDs. These results correspond with the FITR analysis. The fluorescence property and water solubility of the prepared e-CDs can be due to the existence of surface active groups of e-CDs [37,38].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of e-CDs displayed two obvious absorption peaks at 340 and 440 nm (Fig. 4 a). The absorption of 340 nm can be assigned to n-π* transition [39]. The broad absorption band at approximately 440 nm could be assigned to the coupling of the π-state, which is associated with electronic conjugate structures [26]. However, the b-CDs only show an obvious absorption at 340 nm and no typical absorption peak at 440 nm (Fig. S2). It suggests that the EDTA participation has great effect on the electronic conjugate structures and improve the fluorescence performance of green emission band. From Fig. 4b, we can see that the emission of the e-CDs aqueous was at 513 nm and optimal excitation wavelength was at 450 nm, and the QYs of the e-CDs can reach 31.2%.

The fluorescent stability of the prepared e-CDs was also studied systematically under various conditions before real applications. Under the irradiation of excitation light, the PL intensity of the e-CDs decreased less than 3% within 120 min (Fig. S3). From Fig. S4, we can see that the fluorescence of the e-CDs was almost unchanged even at a NaCl concentration of 1 mol⋅L−1. Furthermore, the prepared e-CDs were temperature-insensitive, and the PL intensity of e-CDs was almost constant at 20 °C −45 °C (Fig. S5). All of the above results indicate that the fluorescent property of e-CDs is rather stable and suitable for further sensing applications.

The prepared e-CDs can be used as an effective colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cu2+. From Fig. 5 a, the e-CDs showed an obvious absorption at 440 nm as a yellow solution. After Cu2+ added, the intensity of 440 nm increased with color change from yellow to brown (Fig. 5a, inset). Furthermore, the e-CD aqueous solution emitted bright green fluorescence (Fig. 5b, inset). However, the PL intensity of the e-CDs decreased obviously and emitted a faint green fluorescence after Cu2+ was added. In addition, the fluorescence of prepared e-CDs decreased rapidly in the initial stage and plateaued after approximately 15 min (Fig. S6).

The possible mechanism of Cu2+ sensing in colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode was also discussed in detail. In a previous report, the o-phenylenediamine was selected as raw material for the preparation of o-CDs through hydrothermal treatment. The aromatic amino groups of the obtained o-CDs exhibit unique coordination capability with Cu2+ [34]. Similar to the o-CDs, the surface of the e-CDs also possesses abundant aromatic amino groups which can coordinate with Cu2+. On the other hand, He et al. have demonstrated that strong intermolecular forces can cause aggregation of m-PD-grafted CDs as concentration increases [40]. Therefore, we deduce that aggregation of the e-CDs also occurred in proposed system with the induction of Cu2+, which can lead to the increasement of absorption intensity. As shown in Fig. 5a, after Cu2+ was added, the absorption of 440 nm increased notably. The quenching mechanism can be explained from following aspects. Firstly, the quenching should be due to the energy transfer and electron transfer effect of cupric amine complexes, which is similar to other types of CDs with abundant amino groups [41,42]. Secondly, internal filtration effect may be another important source of fluorescence quenching, because the excitation wavelength (450 nm) of e-CDs locates exactly within the absorption spectrum of Cu2+-e-CDs complex (400–500 nm). Besides, the participation of EDTA could increase surface oxygenic functional groups of e-CDs, so the coordination reaction between Cu2+ and O atoms of e-CDs might be conducive to the PL quenching of e-CDs to some extent [43,44]. Given these effects, the prepared e-CDs can be used as an effective and sensitive probe for the Cu2+ analysis.

The determination of Cu2+ can be realized through colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode. First, the PL intensity of the e-CDs decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence change is described as ΔI/I0, where ΔI is the variation of PL intensity before (I0) and after (I) the addition of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 6b, the quenching efficiency (ΔI/I0) exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ at 0.1–100 μM. The correlation coefficient was 0.9997 and the limit of detection (LOD) of fluorescence mode was 21 nM for Cu2+ (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the colorimetric analysis of Cu2+ was also investigated systematically (Fig. 6c, d). The absorption value changes and the concentrations of Cu2+ were described as A/A0, where A0 and A were the absorption value of 440 nm of the e-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ respectively. The linear range was 1–150 μM with a LOD of 0.3 μM (Fig. 6d). The correlation coefficient was 0.9941. After adding Cu2+ with different concentrations, the color of the aqueous e-CD solution can change from yellow to brown gradually and can be distinguished by the naked eye under natural light (Fig. 6c inset). According to the USEPA, the maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG) for Cu2+ in drinking water is 1.3 mg L−1 (~20 μM) to protect human health against exposure to excessive Cu2+ [45]. The LODs of the methods are much lower than the permitted value of Cu2+ (~20 μM). Hence, colorimetric analysis can be a rapid and sensitive method to evaluate whether the concentration of Cu2+ exceeded the regulatory limit. And the quantitative analysis of low concentration also can be realized with the aid of UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer. For further analysis or trace determination, the fluorescent method can provide sufficient sensitivity. The analytical performance of proposed methods also was compared with previous reported CDs in sensing Cu2+. As shown in Table S1, the sensitivity of proposed sensor was comparable with previous reported works both in fluorescent and colorimetric modes.

As the real sample is complex, the validity of the optical sensor was also evaluated through competition experiments. The selectivity of the e-CDs probe for Cu2+ was carefully studied against other heavy metal cations, including Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+. As shown in Fig. 7 a, only Cu2+ can cause obvious signal response in fluorescence mode, indicating the high selectivity of the designed probe for Cu2+. Furthermore, the selectivity test was also performed in colorimetric mode. In Fig. 7b inset, other heavy metal cations cannot obviously change the color of e-CDs solution. Only Cu2+ can increase the absorption of 440 nm markedly (Fig. 7b). The excellent selectivity was derived from the high affinity of Cu2+ toward the amine groups on the surface of e-CDs. Cu2+ can make a complex with the nitrogen lone pair on the amino group by electron pair sharing [42]. Competitive experiments also indicated that the association ability of amines on the surface of e-CDs with Cu2+ is higher than that of other metal ions. Therefore, the proposed dual-readout sensor can be used for the selective detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed sensor for the determination of Cu2+ was evaluated by several certified reference materials, river water (collected from Yellow River, Jinan) and urine samples. As shown in Table 1 , the determined concentrations of the certified reference materials through the fluorescence method are consistent with the calibration values. The urine samples were subjected to 100-fold dilution before analysis. Pearl River water and diluted urine samples were spiked and analyzed by fluorescent method. We can see from Table 2 , the recoveries of all samples were 98.6–106.2%, and the RSDs were below 4.4%. The above results further confirm the reliability and applicability of the probe for the analysis of Cu2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of Cu2+ and fiber staining
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed sensors for biological application was evaluated through intracellular imaging. The cytotoxicity of the prepared e-CDs was first verified using SMMC7721 hepatoma cells and the MTT assay. Fig. S8 indicated that the cell viability was over 87%, even the concentration of e-CDs was at 50 μg mL−1. High cell viability confirms that e-CDs have low cytotoxicity and can be a suitable candidate for monitoring Cu2+ in living cells.

The prepared e-CDs also exhibited high stability against photobleaching and response to Cu2+. Therefore, cell imaging experiments were performed to verify whether they can be used in biological systems (Fig. 8 ). After incubating the SMMC 7721 hepatoma with the e-CDs, an obvious green fluorescence signal from the intracellular region was observed with blue light excitation (Fig. 8b, e), which indicates that the e-CDs are membrane-permeable. Next, additional Cu2+ was introduced into the e-CDs stained SMMC7721 hepatoma cells. After incubation in the growth medium at 37 °C for another 1 h, the microscope images only showed very weak intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 8c, f), which suggested that the proposed sensor can be applied for the intracellular imaging of Cu2+.

Considering the good fluorescence performance and water solubility, the prepared e-CDs also was tried for fiber staining. The absorbent cotton was soaked with the aqueous solution of e-CDs for 30 min, rinsed and dried at 40 °C. As shown in Fig. 9 a–c, the absorbent cotton only exhibited weak blue fluorescence when used ultraviolet light as excitation, and no obvious fluorescence signal can be captured when exposed to blue and green light. As can be seen in Fig. 9d–f, after being stained, the absorbent cotton emitted green and red fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet/blue and green light excitation respectively. The red fluorescence is due to the aggregation of e-CDs caused by strong intermolecular forces [40,46], which is also consistent with the emission spectrum of high concentration e-CDs solution excited by green light (Fig. S8). All these results indicate that e-CDs can be absorbed to the surface of cotton and maintain excellent solid fluorescence. Therefore, the prepared e-CDs possess great potential for being used as high efficient fluorescent staining reagent.

In summary, we presented an easy and facile strategy for enhancing the fluorescence performance of CDs. Organic acid was used in the water phase preparation of CDs to increase intrinsic electronic conjugate structures and enrich the types of surface functional groups of CDs. The flexible control of the optimal fluorescence band of CDs and enhancement of QYs were realized. The prepared e-CDs with high QY have been employed to the sensitive and selective analysis of Cu2+ successfully through a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout assay. The proposed probe can be used to achieve fast judgment and trace determination of Cu2+ simultaneously. More importantly, the established nanosensor displayed low cytotoxicity and high selectivity and possessed a potential in biological application. The key point of our research is the modulation of fluorescent properties through organic acids and dual-readout sensing of Cu2+ by single CDs, which is beneficial to the reasonable application of nanomaterials in the field of sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189) and School-level College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program of Qilu University of Technology (xj201810431012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.144567.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper signaling in the mammalian nervous system: synaptic effects
J. Neurosci. Res.

Plant Physiol.

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel “turn-on” fluorometric and magnetic bi-functional strategy for ascorbic acid sensing and in vivo imaging via carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheet nanoprobe
